what_manipulated: immigration concern and country-level immigration history
what_measured: political trust in institutions and politicians
experiment: Multilevel hierarchical linear modeling tested whether individual-level concern about immigration and country-level contextual factors (long-term postwar immigration history, far-right party popularity) predict political distrust across European nations. Three indicators of trust (parliament, politicians, legal system) measured on 0-10 scales. Analysis incorporated four waves of European Social Survey with individual-level, country-round-level, and country-level factors; controlled for economic perceptions, social capital, governance, welfare spending, and electoral outcomes.

what_measured: task switching costs: global switch cost and local switch cost (reaction time)
experiment: Young adults performed a computerized task-switching paradigm classifying colored animal pictures by either color or shape per trial cue. All trials were response-incompatible (100% remapping required). Global switch cost calculated as reaction time difference between non-switch trials in mixed-task vs. single-task blocks. Local switch cost calculated as reaction time difference between switch vs. non-switch trials within mixed-task blocks. Median reaction times of correct, correctly-preceded trials analyzed via ANOVA.

what_manipulated: perceived rarity of observed event outcome
what_measured: estimated number of prior trials preceding observed event
experiment: Participants observed a single rare or common event and estimated in open-ended format how many trials preceded it. Study 1: coin flips all heads vs. mixed heads/tails. Study 2: die rolls three 6's vs. two 6's vs. mixed (between-subjects) and within-subject replication. Study 3: 15 real-world scenarios (e.g., lottery win, becoming pregnant) presented with rare or common outcome. Log-transformed estimates analyzed via mixed-effect models. Perceived likelihood of outcome measured post-hoc as mediator.

what_manipulated: regret salience manipulation and external accountability demand
what_measured: choice of target vs. non-target option; decision regret and justifiability ratings
experiment: Participants chose from three-option sets (job or candidate) containing decoy dominated by target but not non-target. Between-subjects conditions: control (no manipulation), regret-salient (paragraph emphasizing regret possibility), accountability (told will write and be evaluated on explanation). Primary dependent variable: binary choice of target vs. non-target. Decoy effect operationalized as proportion choosing target > 50%. Secondary measures: imagined regret and justifiability (1-7 scales) if outcome unsatisfactory. Written decision accounts coded for option mentions and arguments.

what_manipulated: type of information correction to political innuendo
what_measured: favorability toward politician; belief in bribery allegation; belief in investigation rumor
experiment: Participants read sequential news dispatches (9-11 per condition) about fictional Alaskan state senator Don Swensen's sudden resignation. Block-randomized conditions: control (resignation only), innuendo (linked to corruption investigation), denial (innuendo + prosecutor letter stating no investigation), causal correction (denial + alternate explanation: university presidency appointment). Outcomes measured on Likert scales post-distraction: six-point favorability, five-point bribery belief, four-point investigation likelihood. Analysis: OLS with inverse probability weights and survey weights.

what_manipulated: activation of gender stereotype via subtlety and explicitness
what_measured: political knowledge test score; don't-know response rate; items attempted
experiment: Study 2: Students took a 20-item timed political knowledge test (20 seconds per item, five-point response scale including don't-know option). Between-subjects stereotype activation: none (control, neutral instructions), subtle (asked to indicate gender before test), explicit (instructions stated test shows gender differences in past). Design: 2 (gender) × 2 (field of study: psychology vs. politics) × 3 (stereotype condition) ANCOVA with political interest as covariate. Primary outcome: test score (0-20). Secondary: count of don't-know responses and items attempted.
